title: Equivalent Expressions

projects.title: Unit 6 Project — Expressions in Action
projects.desc: Capstone: apply exponents, expressions, and the distributive property to a real-world project.

projects.title: Equivalent Expressions Forge
projects.desc: Generate and verify equivalent algebraic expressions.

projects.title: Expression Engine
projects.desc: Build, evaluate, and match algebraic expressions in an interactive game.

contentObjective: I can show that two expressions are equivalent by simplifying and combining like terms.
languageObjective: I can explain my work using the words equivalent, simplify, like terms, and combine.

turnAndTalk.question: Producer A wrote 3x + 2x + 10 and Producer B wrote 5x + 10. They look different but both give 30 when x = 4. Why are they equivalent?
turnAndTalk.kernel: 3x + 2x combines to 5x, so the two forms are equivalent.

stems.en: I checked by substituting x = ___ and getting ___ both ways.
stems.es: Lo comprobé sustituyendo x = ___ y obteniendo ___ en ambas.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students explain 3x + 2x = 5x (combining like terms), so both equal 5x + 10, and verify by substituting a value like x = 4 to get 30 both ways.
turnAndTalk.extend: Is testing only x = 4 enough to be sure two expressions are equivalent? Justify why or why not.
extendStems: Testing one value is ___ enough because ___.
To be sure they are equivalent, I need ___.

turnAndTalk.question: How can you prove 2(n + 4) and 2n + 8 are equivalent? What two strategies could you use?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Distribute or substitute values to show the forms always match.

stems.en: They are equivalent because distributing 2(n + 4) gives ___.
I can also check by substituting n = ___ and getting ___.
stems.es: También puedo comprobar sustituyendo n = ___ y obteniendo ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: A strong answer uses the distributive property (2(n + 4) = 2n + 8) and/or substitutes a value into both to confirm equal results.
turnAndTalk.extend: Are 2x + 3 and 5x equivalent? Find a value of x that proves your answer and explain why one test value showing equality is not enough.
extendStems: They are ___ equivalent because at x = ___ they give ___ and ___.
One matching value is not proof because ___.

turnAndTalk.question: Client A pays 4h + 2h + 30 and Client B pays 6h + 30. Is the pricing fair (equivalent)? How can you prove it?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Combine 4h + 2h to 6h, showing both clients pay the same.

stems.en: I can prove it by substituting h = ___: both equal ___.
stems.es: Puedo probarlo sustituyendo h = ___: ambas dan ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students simplify 4h + 2h + 30 to 6h + 30, conclude the pricing is fair/equivalent, and verify by substituting a value of h.
turnAndTalk.extend: A third client pays 5h + 30. Critique whether that is the same deal, and explain for which client it is cheaper as hours grow.
extendStems: As hours grow, the ___ client pays less because the coefficient of h is ___.

turnAndTalk.question: During practice on Equivalent Expressions, what strategy did you use when a problem felt tricky?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Equivalent expressions give the same answer for every value of the variable.

stems.en: They are equivalent because distributing 2(n + 4) gives ___.
I knew my answer was reasonable because ___.
stems.es: Supe que mi respuesta era razonable porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Listen for students naming a specific strategy tied to 6.EE.4 — not just "I multiplied." They should connect steps to the key idea.
turnAndTalk.extend: How would you help a classmate who made the common mistake from today's lesson? Use one vocabulary word.
extendStems: I would tell them to ___ first because ___.
The mistake happens when you ___ instead of ___.

vocabulary.definition: Expressions that always have the same value.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Expresiones que siempre tienen el mismo valor.
vocabulary.visual: 2x + 4 and 2(x + 2): when x = 3, both = 10; when x = 7, both = 18 — always the same

vocabulary.definition: To write an expression in a shorter, simpler way.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Escribir una expresión de forma más corta y simple.
vocabulary.visual: 3x + 2x + 5 simplifies to 5x + 5 — fewer terms, same value
vocabulary.cloze: To write an expression in its shortest equal form is to ___ it.

vocabulary.definition: Terms with the same letter, like 2x and 5x.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Términos con la misma letra, como 2x y 5x.
vocabulary.visual: 3x and 7x are like terms (both x); 3x and 3y are NOT (x vs y); 3x and 3x² are NOT (x vs x²)
vocabulary.cloze: Terms with the same variable raised to the same power, like 4x and 9x, are ___.

examples.why: Both have the same variable x to the first power.
One has a variable and one does not, so they are not like terms.

vocabulary.definition: To add or subtract terms with the same letter.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Sumar o restar términos con la misma letra.
vocabulary.visual: 4n + 3n = 7n — add the coefficients (4 + 3 = 7) and keep the variable (n)
vocabulary.cloze: To add or subtract like terms into one term is to ___ them.

vocabulary.definition: The number in front of a letter, like the 3 in 3x.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El número frente a una letra, como el 3 en 3x.
vocabulary.visual: In 5x + 3, the coefficient of x is 5 — when x = 2, the 5x part equals 10
vocabulary.cloze: A number multiplied by a variable is a ___.

launch.narrative: At the music studio, two producers wrote formulas for the cost of renting equipment. Producer A wrote 3x + 2x + 10, and Producer B wrote 5x + 10. They look different, but are they really? Let's check: if x = 4, Producer A gets 3(4) + 2(4) + 10 = 30, and Producer B gets 5(4) + 10 = 30. Same result — they're equivalent expressions!
launch.contextImage: Two music producers at mixing boards comparing different but equivalent formulas
noticePrompts: Why did both formulas give the same answer?
What did Producer B do differently from Producer A?
Would they still match if x = 10?
wonderPrompts: Can two expressions look completely different but always give the same answer?
Is there always a 'simplest' way to write an expression?

conceptIntro.heading: When are two expressions equivalent?
conceptIntro.intro: Two expressions are equivalent when they always give the same value, no matter what number you put in for the variable. They can look different but mean the same thing.
conceptIntro.keyIdea: Equivalent expressions give the same answer for every value of the variable.

iDo.title: Watch me
lines: Producer A wrote 3x + 2x + 10 and Producer B wrote 5x + 10. Are they equivalent?
Both give 30, and 3x + 2x is just 5x, so they are equivalent.

youDo.title: Now it's your turn
lines: Sort each expression into the group with its equivalent match.
Combine like terms or test a value to check.

explore.instructions: Sort each expression into the group with its equivalent expression.

discourse.prompt: How did you decide which expressions belong in the same group? What strategies did you use?
discourse.sentenceFrame: I knew ___ and ___ are equivalent because when I ___ the terms, I got the same expression.

optionalActivity.name: Equivalent Expression Match Battle
optionalActivity.intro: In the remix studio, two producers claim their beat formulas are the same — spin the tracks to prove which expressions are truly equivalent!

optional.stem: Producer A wrote 2(x + 3) for a beat's cost. Which expression is equivalent?
optional.explanation: Distribute the 2 to each term inside: 2 · x + 2 · 3 = 2x + 6. Check x = 5: 2(5 + 3) = 16 and 2(5) + 6 = 16.

optional.stem: A track stacks 3x and then one more x. Which expression is equivalent to 3x + x?
optional.explanation: 3x + x is 3 groups of x plus 1 more group of x = 4x. Add the coefficients: 3 + 1 = 4. Check x = 3: 3(3) + 3 = 12 and 4(3) = 12.

optional.stem: Producer B flipped a formula to 5 + 2x. Which expression is equivalent?
optional.explanation: Order of addition does not change the value (commutative property): 5 + 2x = 2x + 5. Check x = 10: 5 + 2(10) = 25 and 2(10) + 5 = 25.

optional.instructions: The target beat formula is 4x + 6. Spin each expression onto the deck where it belongs: equivalent to 4x + 6, or NOT equivalent.

optional.label: Match each studio beat formula to its equivalent remix.

optional.title: Level 2 Extension — The False Remix

workedExample.label: Claim
workedExample.work: 2x and 6 are like terms, so combine them

optional.correctWork: 2x and 6 are NOT like terms — 2x has a variable and 6 is just a number, so they cannot be combined into one term. 2x + 6 is already simplified. You could rewrite it by factoring: 2x + 6 = 2(x + 3). Check x = 5: 2(5) + 6 = 16, but 8(5) = 40 — not equal, so 8x is wrong.
hints: Are 2x and 6 like terms? One has a variable and one does not.
Try substituting x = 5 into 2x + 6 and into 8x. Do you get the same value?

approaching.explanation: Multiplying 3 by each term inside the parentheses is the distributive property.
5n + 2n = 7n. Add the coefficients: 5 + 2 = 7. The variable stays as n.

approaching.instructions: Sort each pair of expressions by whether they are equivalent or not.

approaching.title: approaching — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Equivalent Expressions.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: Equivalent expressions give the same answer for every value of the variable.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

approaching.correctWork: Follow the key idea: Equivalent expressions give the same answer for every value of the variable.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: Equivalent expressions give the same answer for every value of the variable.

onLevel.instructions: Match each expression with its simplified form.

onLevel.label: Match each expression to its simplified equivalent.

onLevel.instructions: Simplify each expression. Then evaluate both the original and simplified form when x = 3 to verify they are equivalent.
Are these equivalent? Test with x = 2. Left: 3x + 2x + 4. Right: 5x + 4.
onLevel.label: Both equal 14 — they are equivalent because 3x + 2x = 5x.

onLevel.title: onLevel — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Equivalent Expressions.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: Equivalent expressions give the same answer for every value of the variable.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

onLevel.correctWork: Follow the key idea: Equivalent expressions give the same answer for every value of the variable.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: Equivalent expressions give the same answer for every value of the variable.

extending.title: Find the Simplification Error
extending.correctWork: 3x + 2x = 5x (NOT 5x²). When you combine like terms, you add the coefficients, not multiply them. The correct answer is 5x + 6.
hints: When you combine 3x + 2x, do you add the exponents or the coefficients?
3x means 3 groups of x. 2x means 2 groups of x. Together that's ___ groups of x.

extending.prompt: A student says 2x + 3 and 5x are equivalent because both equal 5 when x = 1. Is the student correct? Explain why testing one value is not enough to prove equivalence, and find a value of x where they give different results.
extending.sentenceFrame: The student is ___ because ___. When x = 1: 2(1) + 3 = ___ and 5(1) = ___. But when x = ___: 2(___) + 3 = ___ and 5(___) = ___. To prove equivalence, expressions must be equal for ___ values of x.

extending.instructions: For each expression, find an equivalent expression using a DIFFERENT method (distributive property, combining like terms, or factoring).

practice.commonMistake: A common mistake in Equivalent Expressions is skipping the key idea: "Equivalent expressions give the same answer for every value of the variable." — always check your work against this rule before you submit.

connect.scenario: A music studio charges different clients using different formulas. Client A pays 4h + 2h + 30 for h hours. Client B pays 6h + 30. A new client asks if the pricing is fair.
connect.promptQuestion: Are these expressions equivalent? How can you prove it?
connect.prompt: The expressions ___ equivalent because when I simplify 4h + 2h + 30, I get ___. I can prove it by substituting h = ___: both expressions equal ___.

exitTicket.explanation: 6n + 3n = 9n and 4 − 1 = 3, so the simplified expression is 9n + 3.
